Every JavaScript program runs inside an execution context with two phases: memory creation and code execution.
During memory creation, variables are initialized as undefined and functions are stored entirely in memory.
The memory component is also called the Variable Environment.
The code component executes your program line by line - also called the Thread of Execution.
JavaScript is synchronous and single-threaded.
Single-threaded means only one task runs at a time.
Synchronous means execution happens in a predictable, ordered sequence.
The Global Execution Context always sits at the bottom of the call stack.
The call stack maintains the order of execution contexts.
Hoisting allows access to declarations before execution begins - but not before initialization.
Before execution, JavaScript scans the entire program and allocates memory.
Variables are assigned undefined during hoisting as a placeholder value.
Function declarations are fully hoisted with their definitions.
Function expressions and arrow functions behave like variables during hoisting.
undefined is a placeholder value; not defined means the variable was never declared.
JavaScript is dynamically typed - variable types are determined at runtime.
Code outside any function runs in the global scope.
In browsers, the global object is window.
At the global level, this refers to the window object in browsers.
Scope defines where variables and functions are accessible.
A lexical environment consists of local memory and a reference to its parent environment.
The chain of lexical environments forms the scope chain.
let and const are hoisted but stored in a separate memory space.
The Temporal Dead Zone exists between hoisting and initialization of let and const.
Accessing a variable in the Temporal Dead Zone throws a ReferenceError.
A block is a compound statement wrapped in curly braces.
Blocks allow multiple statements where JavaScript expects one.
let and const are block-scoped; var is function-scoped.
Shadowing occurs when an inner variable shares the same name as an outer variable.
Illegal shadowing happens when scope rules are violated.
A closure is a function bundled with its lexical environment.
Returned functions retain access to variables from their outer scope.
Closures store references to variables, not copies of their values.
Closures power patterns like currying, memoization, and module design.
Anonymous functions are often used where functions are treated as values.
Functions are first-class citizens in JavaScript.
Functions can be passed as arguments and returned from other functions.
Parameters are placeholders in function definitions; arguments are actual values passed.
A callback is a function passed into another function.
Callbacks enable asynchronous behavior.
The main thread is the call stack - avoid blocking it.
The event loop moves tasks from queues to the call stack when it’s empty.
The microtask queue has higher priority than the callback queue.
Promises are scheduled in the microtask queue.
Long chains of microtasks can cause task starvation.
setTimeout(..., 0) defers execution until the current stack clears.
A JavaScript engine processes code in three stages: parsing, compilation, and execution.
Modern engines use Just-In-Time compilation for performance.
The parser converts code into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
V8 uses Ignition as its interpreter and TurboFan as its optimizing compiler.
JavaScript doesn’t move code during hoisting - it allocates memory before execution begins.
The call stack only executes what is on top. Everything else waits.
The event loop doesn’t run code - it coordinates when code can run.
Asynchronous JavaScript is not multi-threading; it’s structured waiting.
Promises don’t execute immediately - they schedule microtasks.
Microtasks always run before macrotasks after the current stack clears.
If the call stack is busy, nothing else can run - not even setTimeout.
Closures are not magic - they are just scope preserved over time.
JavaScript’s power comes from functions being both values and behavior.
Every function invocation creates a new execution context.
Scope is resolved at write time, not at run time.
Lexical scope is about where code is written, not where it is executed.
this is not about where a function is defined - it’s about how it is called.
Arrow functions inherit this from their surrounding scope.
The prototype chain is how JavaScript answers the question: where do I find this property?
Objects don’t copy properties through inheritance - they delegate lookup.
Immutability reduces side effects and increases predictability.
Blocking the main thread blocks the user experience.
JavaScript concurrency is managed, not parallel by default.
Garbage collection frees memory that is no longer reachable.
Everything in JavaScript is either a primitive value or an object reference.
Modules prevent global pollution and enforce scope boundaries.
Async/await is syntax - the engine still works with Promises underneath.
Understanding the event loop removes most async confusion.
Performance in JavaScript often depends on how predictable your objects are.
The engine optimizes patterns it can predict.
JavaScript engines don’t just interpret - they compile and optimize.
Hidden classes help engines optimize object property access.
Inline caching makes repeated property access faster.
Understanding fundamentals makes debugging 10x easier.
